meta.course: STAT 252 — Statistical Inference for Management II
meta.concept_tested: One-Way ANOVA: Within & Between Group Variability
meta.notes: Pretest administered last 10 min of class on 4/27. Posttest administered during experiment on 4/29 after activity. Questions are conceptual — short answer graded for understanding, not exact phrasing.

pretest.title: Pre-Test: One-Way ANOVA (Prior Knowledge)
pretest.instructions: Answer each question in your own words. These questions are about what you already know — not what you'll learn today. There are no trick questions. If you don't know, write what you think.

questions.question: In your own words, what is the goal of a one-way ANOVA test? What question is it trying to answer?

rubric.2: Correctly states ANOVA tests whether group means are equal / whether groups differ on average
rubric.1: Partially correct — mentions comparing groups but unclear on means or multiple groups
rubric.0: No response or completely incorrect (e.g., describes correlation or regression)

questions.question: You observe three groups of students who used different study methods. Group A averaged 72 on an exam, Group B averaged 85, Group C averaged 78. The overall class average was 78.3. What does the spread of these group averages (72, 85, 78) tell you about the data?

rubric.2: Interprets spread of group means as evidence of potential group differences; mentions between-group idea even without the term
rubric.1: Notes that the groups differ but gives no interpretive reasoning

questions.question: Within Group A (avg = 72), individual scores ranged from 55 to 89. Within Group C (avg = 78), scores ranged from 75 to 82. What does this difference in spread tell you?

rubric.2: Identifies that Group A has more variability within the group; connects to idea of within-group spread or noise even without the term
rubric.1: Notes groups differ in spread but gives no interpretation

questions.question: In a one-way ANOVA, the F-statistic is calculated as a ratio. Which ratio is correct?
Can the F-statistic ever be negative? Briefly explain why or why not.

rubric.2: Correctly states F cannot be negative AND gives a valid reason (e.g., it's a ratio of variances / sums of squares which are always ≥ 0)
rubric.1: Correctly states F cannot be negative but gives no reason or a weak reason
rubric.0: States F can be negative, or no response

posttest.title: Post-Test: One-Way ANOVA — Within & Between Group Variability
posttest.instructions: Answer each question in your own words based on what you learned during today's activity. Be specific — vague answers will receive partial credit only.

questions.question: In your own words, define within-group variability. What does it measure, and what role does it play in one-way ANOVA?

rubric.2: Correctly defines within-group variability as spread of individual observations around their group mean; mentions it represents noise or is the denominator of F
rubric.1: Partially correct — defines spread within groups but unclear on role in ANOVA
rubric.0: No response or confused with between-group variability

questions.question: In your own words, define between-group variability. How is it different from within-group variability?

rubric.2: Correctly defines between-group variability as spread of group means around the grand mean; clearly distinguishes from within-group (individual deviations from own group mean)
rubric.1: Defines one correctly but conflates the two
rubric.0: No response or both defined incorrectly

questions.question: A researcher runs a one-way ANOVA and gets F = 8.4. Another researcher gets F = 0.9. Interpret both values: what does each tell you about the relationship between group means?

rubric.3: F = 8.4 interpreted as strong evidence of group differences (between >> within); F = 0.9 interpreted as no evidence of group differences (similar to H₀ being true); explanation references signal/noise ratio or numerator/denominator logic
rubric.2: Both interpreted correctly but reasoning is incomplete
rubric.0: No response or both interpreted incorrectly

questions.question: Can within-group variability be different across groups in ANOVA? What condition does ANOVA require regarding within-group variability, and why does it matter?

rubric.1: States that ANOVA assumes roughly equal within-group variability (homogeneity of variance) AND gives a reason why (pooling MSE, or ability to fairly compare groups)
rubric.0: No response, or states no condition is needed

questions.question: Reflect on your learning today. Describe one thing that was clearer after the activity than before, and one thing you're still uncertain about regarding one-way ANOVA.

rubric.2: Provides a specific, substantive reflection on both what was learned and what remains unclear — not generic
rubric.1: Only addresses one part, or both responses are too vague

questions.notes: This question also serves as qualitative data for the study. Do not penalize for honest uncertainty.
